skill.name: rust-cargo-assistant
skill.description: Guide Rust developers through Cargo project setup, dependency management, workspaces, builds, tests, benchmarks, and crate publishing.
skill.summary: Practical Cargo guidance for Rust project configuration and crate workflows.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell-command, URL, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are Cargo documentation examples, not executable skill code, but the skill can recommend real commands including sudo package installs and crate publishing steps. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, malware intent, or hidden automation was found.

medium_findings.title: Operational Shell Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk. SKILL.md contains many bash examples for Cargo actions such as creating projects, building, running, testing, installing tools, removing lock files, logging in to crates.io, and publishing crates. These are legitimate for a Cargo assistant, but users may execute commands that modify projects, install binaries, or publish packages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in bash documentation blocks and match the skill purpose. Risk is operational rather than malicious because no hidden execution path or exfiltration behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Package Installation Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for elevated-permission command guidance. The skill suggests sudo apt package installation for compiler and linker setup. This is a normal troubleshooting pattern, but privileged package installs can affect the host system and should require user review before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are explicit and semantically tied to installing build tools. They are not malicious, but they do request elevated system changes.

medium_findings.title: Crates.io Token and Publishing Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for sensitive workflow guidance. The publishing section includes cargo login with an API token placeholder and cargo publish commands. The token is not hardcoded, but the workflow handles credentials and public package release actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential placeholder and publish commands are clear, but no evidence shows token collection, storage, or transmission by the skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Examples Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious networking. The detected URLs are documentation, homepage, repository, and sample git dependency values in Cargo.toml examples. They point to docs.rs or placeholder GitHub paths and do not perform network requests by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are inside Cargo.toml examples and are visibly placeholder project metadata. No fetch, upload, webhook, or external callback code was found.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Paths Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for path traversal and temporary directory abuse. The relative path shows a local Cargo path dependency, and the temporary directory line sets a Cargo target directory. These examples do not read arbitrary files or escape a sandbox.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both paths appear in documented Cargo configuration examples. There is no code that joins untrusted paths or accesses sensitive filesystem locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for weak cryptography. The flagged lines contain front matter and Cargo package description fields, not cryptographic functions or algorithms. No evidence found for MD5, SHA-1, custom encryption, or insecure key handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct review of the flagged lines shows only descriptive text fields. The static pattern does not correspond to any cryptographic operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged System Package Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes sudo apt and sudo apt-get examples. These commands should be treated as user-approved system changes, not automatic actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The elevated commands are explicitly documented. The surrounding context is legitimate troubleshooting guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Publishing and Credential Handling Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents cargo login and cargo publish. These commands can expose account tokens through misuse or publish packages if run without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real and sensitive, but the token is a placeholder and no exfiltration path is present.

content.value_statement: Rust teams need consistent Cargo setup, dependency management, and publishing workflows. This skill provides structured guidance for Cargo.toml, workspaces, crates, tests, builds, and releases.
actual_capabilities: Explains Cargo project initialization for binaries, libraries, and existing directories.
Guides Cargo.toml configuration for packages, dependencies, features, profiles, binaries, libraries, and workspaces.
Provides dependency management patterns for crates.io, git, path, optional, dev, build, and target-specific dependencies.
Covers build, test, benchmark, documentation, formatting, linting, and troubleshooting commands.
Describes crate packaging and publishing workflows, including dry runs and yanking versions.
Summarizes useful Cargo ecosystem tools such as cargo-edit, cargo-watch, cargo-audit, and cargo-outdated.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a verified execution environment.
Commands may change files, install tools, or publish crates if a user runs them.
It does not validate crate security, license terms, or supply-chain trust by itself.
Some version examples may become outdated as the Rust ecosystem changes.

use_cases.title: Start a New Rust Project
use_cases.description: Set up a binary, library, or workspace with suitable Cargo.toml sections and project structure.

use_cases.title: Resolve Cargo Build Issues
use_cases.description: Diagnose dependency conflicts, linker errors, lock file problems, and slow compilation workflows.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Crate for Release
use_cases.description: Review package metadata, included files, dry-run publishing steps, and version management tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Cargo Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new Rust project with Cargo. Explain whether I should use a binary, library, or workspace.

prompt_templates.title: Review Cargo.toml
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Cargo.toml plan for dependencies, features, profiles, and package metadata. Point out risky or missing settings.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Dependency Conflicts
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me debug a Cargo dependency resolution problem. Explain the likely cause and a safe sequence of checks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Crate Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a release checklist for publishing my Rust crate. Include package validation, dry runs, versioning, and rollback considerations.

output_examples.input: I want to split a growing Rust app into multiple crates.
output: Recommendation to use a workspace with shared dependency versions.
Suggested crate boundaries for core logic, API, and CLI entry points.
Checklist for moving tests, examples, and package metadata.

output_examples.input: Cargo reports duplicate versions of the same dependency.
output: Explanation of how Cargo resolves versions.
Steps to inspect the dependency tree and identify the parent crates.
Options for updating, pinning, or coordinating workspace dependency versions.

output_examples.input: I am preparing my first crate release.
output: Publishing readiness checklist for metadata, README, license, and included files.
Reminder to test packaging before publishing.
Guidance to protect API tokens and review irreversible release actions.

best_practices: Review every generated command before running it, especially install, remove, login, and publish commands.
Use dry-run and verification steps before publishing crates or changing release metadata.
Keep dependency versions, features, and workspace settings explicit enough for repeatable builds.
anti_patterns: Running privileged package installation commands without checking the package source and system impact.
Publishing crates before validating package contents, version numbers, and metadata.
Adding broad dependency features without checking compile time, binary size, and transitive risk.

faq.question: Can this skill create a Rust project for me?
faq.answer: It can guide the project structure and Cargo commands. You should review commands before execution.

faq.question: Does it manage Cargo.toml dependencies?
faq.answer: It explains dependency formats, feature flags, optional dependencies, and workspace dependency patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with build failures?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers common Cargo, linker, dependency resolution, and lock file troubleshooting paths.

faq.question: Is it safe to use for crate publishing?
faq.answer: Use it as a checklist. Publishing and login commands require explicit human review and token protection.

faq.question: Does it audit crates for vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It references cargo-audit style workflows, but it does not replace a current supply-chain security review.

faq.question: Which assistants can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
